title: Making a basic portable workbench from plywood
subtitle: Cut and assemble a knock-down plywood workbench using interlocking half-lap joints
excerpt: How to make a lightweight portable workbench from a single sheet of 18 mm plywood, using interlocking half-lap slots at the trestle crossings so the whole bench assembles and disassembles without screws or clamps.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Site Carpentry. TRADA Wood Information Sheet (plywood in construction). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A joint in which both mating pieces are slotted to exactly half their thickness and half their width at the crossing point, so that they interlock flush on both faces when pushed together. Requires no adhesive or mechanical fixings to hold the joint; friction and the mating geometry resist racking. Used extensively in knock-down workshop furniture and display stands.

content.text: This bench consists of two identical trestle frames and a separate work top, all from 18 mm plywood. The frames stand by themselves because the crossed legs are locked with

at their centres. The work top simply rests on the trestles and can be clamped down for heavy work. Overall size: 1200 mm long × 600 mm wide × 800 mm high, adjust all dimensions to suit your working height and available sheet material.

One full sheet (2440 × 1220 mm) of 18 mm structural plywood or birch ply. The sheet yields the two trestle frames, the top panel, and some waste. Cutting list: two leg panels at 800 × 600 mm (portrait orientation, with a slot cut at mid-height); one top panel at 1200 × 600 mm. Sandpaper 80 and 120 grit for edge finishing.
Step 1, mark and cut the trestle panels

Mark the two trestle panels on the plywood sheet, 800 mm tall × 600 mm wide. Each panel has a slot cut into it at the 400 mm centreline: the slot is 18 mm wide (the thickness of the sheet) and 300 mm deep, centred on the panel width. On one panel the slot runs up from the bottom; on the other it runs down from the top. When they interlock, the bottom of one slot meets the top of the other at the centre crossing. Cut the rectangular blanks with the circular saw and a straightedge. Cut each slot with the jigsaw; drill a clearance hole at the base of each slot for the jigsaw blade before cutting. Check the slot width by test-fitting a scrap of the same plywood, it should slide in firmly without play.
Step 2, cut the leg feet angles

Each trestle panel stands as an X shape when the two leg panels are slotted together. To make the feet sit flat, mark a small angle cut at each of the four foot corners, approximately 10 degrees outward from vertical, sufficient to splay the feet slightly. Mark the angle with the combination square set to 10 degrees and cut with the circular saw or jigsaw. Sand all cut edges smooth with the sanding block.

Push the two leg panels together at their interlocking slots. The crossing should be firm and the legs should splay evenly. If the joint is too loose, cut a thin packing shim from a scrap of ply and glue it to one side of one slot to tighten the fit. Repeat for the second trestle. Lay the top panel across both trestles, spaced 900 mm apart, and press down on the centre to check stability. If the frame racks sideways under load, add a 44 × 44 mm timber stretcher across the bottom of each trestle.

Sand all face edges to remove splinters. A coat of clear wood sealer or exterior paint on all surfaces reduces moisture uptake in a workshop environment. For the work top, apply two coats of clear floor varnish to seal the surface against glue, paint, and oil contamination.

attrs.symptom: Trestle racks sideways under lateral load
attrs.cause: The crossing slot joint is slightly loose or the feet angle is not equal on both sides.
attrs.fix: Tighten the joint with a shim as described, or fit a short horizontal stretcher bar across the bottom pair of legs on each trestle, screwed through the ply.

attrs.symptom: Slot too tight to assemble by hand
attrs.cause: The jigsaw cut was slightly inside the marked line, leaving the slot narrower than the sheet thickness.
attrs.fix: Sand the slot sides with a strip of 80-grit paper wrapped around a thin offcut until the joint slides together with firm hand pressure.
